steps.title: Set up the model structure
steps.content: The sample loses 24% of its mass every 22.96 minutes, so 76% remains each period. An exponential decay model has the form $M = M_0 \cdot r^{t/T}$, where $M_0$ is the starting mass (100 g), $r$ is the fraction remaining per period (0.76), and $T$ is the period length (22.96 min).

steps.title: Graph-check the decay model
steps.content:
The Desmos expression shows a decreasing exponential curve with initial value 100 and a 0.76 multiplier every 22.96 minutes.

  • Choice A — ‘$M = 100(0.24) + 22.96$’: This is a constant, not a function of $t$ at all. It also uses 0.24 (the lost fraction) rather than 0.76 (the remaining fraction), and adds the period length, which makes no dimensional sense. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘$M = 100(0.24)^{\frac{6}{22.96}}$’: This uses a constant exponent and base 0.24, so it is not a function of time and does not model 76% survival per 22.96-minute period. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘$M = 100(0.76)t + 22.96$’: Linear, not exponential — mass would decrease by the same amount each minute rather than by a fixed percentage. Exponential decay cannot be modeled with $t$ appearing only as a multiplier. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘$M = 100(0.76)^{\frac{t}{22.96}}$’: Exponential with the correct base (0.76 = fraction remaining), correct initial value (100 g), and correct period ($t$ is divided by 22.96 so that every 22.96 minutes the exponent increases by 1 and mass is multiplied by 0.76). ✓
That confirms D is correct.
